votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, including the range check, digit extraction, table lookups, and the implication structure (returning True when the precondition is not met).
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, including the tables, index calculations, and string concatenation.
The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification, including the tables, index calculations, list lookups with defaults, string concatenation, and the conditional implication.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both use the same lookup tables for Roman numeral conversion. The Python code computes the same indices (thousands, hundreds, tens, ones) using integer division and modulo operations. The nth function correctly handles out-of-bounds access with a default value. When number is outside [1, 1000], the Coq spec is vacuously true (the implication holds), and Python returns True, matching this behavior. For valid inputs, both compute the same concatenation of Roman numeral parts.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute the same indices for thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones digits using integer division and modulo operations. The nth function correctly handles list indexing with a default value. The tables are identical. The Python code returns True when number is outside [1,1000] (matching Coq's vacuous truth for implications), and checks result equality against the concatenated Roman numeral string when number is in range.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both use the same lookup tables, compute the same indices (thousands, hundreds, tens, ones) using integer division and modulo operations, and concatenate the results. The Python nth function matches Coq's nth behavior with default values. When number is outside [1,1000], the Coq spec is vacuously true (implication with false antecedent), and Python returns True, which is equivalent behavior.

For 1..1000, both compute the same indices via integer division/remainder, use identical tables with nth defaults, and concatenate; for out-of-range inputs, Coq’s implication is vacuously true and Python returns True, so behaviors match.
Matches Coq spec: for 1..1000 uses the same division/mod indices and tables to build the string; outside range returns True, mirroring vacuous truth of the implication.
Implements the same implication: returns True when out of range and otherwise checks the concatenation from the same indexed tables using matching division/modulo semantics for nonnegative inputs.
